name: json-myers merge conformance
description: Conformance suite for any JSON merge implementation that claims `json-myers` semantics. Each case is a (base, patch, expected | throws) triple — apply the patch onto the base and the result must equal expected (deep equality), or the call must throw with the documented code.

markers.$ops: Structural array-operations marker. Object carrying $ops expresses add/remove/move on a base ARRAY.
markers.$remove: Bulk key-removal marker. `{ $remove: ['k1', 'k2'] }` at an object level deletes the listed keys from the merged result. Single key uses a 1-element list.
markers.$identity: Per-array identity field name. Optional. When present in an OpsDiff, the patcher uses this field for smart-key operations within that array (overrides PatchOptions.identity and the default 'id'). Each array-diff may declare its own.
markers.$assertCollection: Per-array collection assertion. Optional. When `true`, the patcher pre-validates the base array as a homogeneous collection (every item is a plain object with the identity field, no duplicates). Throws CollectionAssertionError on violation. Inferred automatically by diffJson when both source and target arrays form valid collections.

reserved_identity_values.:index: Positional identity. When an array-diff carries `$identity: ":index"`, the patcher treats the index itself as the identity — nested-update sibling keys are interpreted as numeric indices (`result[Number(key)]`), `$assertCollection` is silenced, and `$ops` operations apply unchanged. Used for Nd matrices and arrays where position IS the identity. The leading colon marks the value as reserved (no real object field is named `:index`).

op_shape.add: { type: 'add', index: number, item: unknown }  OR  { type: 'add', key: string, index?: number }
op_shape.remove: { type: 'remove', index: number }  OR  { type: 'remove', key: string }
op_shape.move: { type: 'move', from: number, to: number }  OR  { type: 'move', key: string, to: number }

rules: R1 — Arrays in a patch (without `$ops`) ALWAYS replace the base array. Never positional/index merge.
R2 — Type change between base and patch (array↔object↔primitive) → patch replaces base inteiramente.
R3 — Structural recursion only in (object, object) pairs whose patch is NOT itself a `$ops` object.

R4 — `$ops` is the structural-array marker: smart-key mode (`key`) when items have identity; positional mode (`index`) otherwise. `move` is canonical; the `remove key=X` + `add key=X` pair is accepted as syntactic sugar with identical semantics.

R5 — `$remove: [...keys]` at an object level deletes the listed keys from the merged result. Removes are processed BEFORE other entries in the same patch, so `{ $remove: ['a'], a: 9 }` produces `{ a: 9 }` (effectively a reset). `$remove` is scoped to the object level where it appears — to remove deep keys, nest it.
R6 — `$ops` requires the base at that position to be an array. Base of any other type (object, primitive, missing) → throw `OPS_BASE_NOT_ARRAY`. Fail-fast at the boundary.

R7 — Strict mode (`patchJson(base, patch, { strict: true })`) — assume the patch was generated by a real `diffJson(base, ...)`. Any inconsistency between patch and base raises `StrictViolationError` with a documented code (`KEY_NOT_FOUND`, `INDEX_OUT_OF_RANGE`, `KEY_ALREADY_EXISTS`, `MOVE_NO_OP`, `OBJECT_KEY_NOT_FOUND`). Default is permissive (silent-ignore inconsistencies). Cases with a `strict_throws` field are validated in both modes — normal returns `expected`; strict throws the listed code.

R8 — Array-diff discriminator. A diff fragment that targets an ARRAY ALWAYS carries `$ops` (even when empty). A diff that targets an OBJECT NEVER carries `$ops`. Presence of `$ops` is the structural signal `"the base at this position is an array"`. Smart-key updates without any add/remove/move still need `$ops: []` to preserve the discriminator.

R9 — Identity is per-array via wire. The default identity field is `"id"`. Each array-diff MAY declare `$identity: "<field>"` to override locally. The patcher resolution order is: `diff.$identity` → `PatchOptions.identity` → `"id"`. There is no `key` fallback (v2 dual-field behavior is removed). Identity values are accepted as `string` or `number`; other types fall back to content hash.

R10 — Collection assertion. An array-diff MAY carry `$assertCollection: true` to assert that the base array is a homogeneous collection. The patcher pre-validates the base (every item is a non-null plain object with the identity field, no duplicates) and throws `CollectionAssertionError` on the first violation: `COLLECTION_NON_OBJECT_ITEM`, `COLLECTION_MISSING_IDENTITY`, or `COLLECTION_DUPLICATE_IDENTITY`. Always thrown (independent of `strict` mode) — like R6, this is a STRUCTURAL violation.

R11 — Positional identity (`$identity: ":index"`). Reserved identity value for Nd matrices and any array where the INDEX is the identity. In positional mode the patcher routes nested-update sibling keys by numeric index (`result[Number(key)]`) instead of by smart-key lookup. Non-integer / negative / fractional / out-of-range sibling keys behave like missed smart-key lookups (silent skip in normal mode, `KEY_NOT_FOUND` in strict mode). `$assertCollection` is silenced in positional mode (a positional array is structurally not a homogeneous-identity collection). Positional `$ops` (`{type:'add', index, item}`, `{type:'remove', index}`, `{type:'move', from, to}`) operate unchanged and compose with cell editing. Recursion is genuine — Nd matrices nest by repeating `$identity: ":index"` at each level.

migration_from_v1.$__remove: replaced by $remove: [...keys] — bulk list form, scoped to object level
migration_from_v1.remove_op_item: remove ops no longer carry redundant `item` field — index/key suffices
migration_from_v1.move_op_item: move ops no longer carry redundant `item` field — from/key + to suffices
migration_from_v1.move_smart_key: new — `{type:'move', key:'X', to:N}` is the canonical smart-key move (remove+add still accepted as sugar)

migration_from_v2.identity_fallback: v2 used `obj.id ?? obj.key` (id wins, key as fallback). v3 uses a single configurable identity field (default 'id'). To use a different identity, declare $identity in the array-diff wire, or pass PatchOptions.identity / DiffOptions.identity.
migration_from_v2.extractSeed: Add ops by smart-key now include the FULL source item in the sibling seed (including the identity field). Preserves the original type of the identity value (e.g. `id: 123` stays a number after patch).
migration_from_v2.duplicate_smart_keys: When an array carries two items with the same smart-key, the FIRST wins (gets the smart-key fingerprint) and the rest fall back to content hash. Round-trip is now correct even with duplicate keys.

cases.description: Two empty objects → empty object
Patch adds new key
Patch overrides existing key with a primitive
Recursive merge — additive at nested level
Recursive merge — override at nested level
Three-level nested recursion
Empty base, full patch — patch wins
Full base, empty patch — base preserved
R1 — Shorter patch array replaces longer base array
R1 — Longer patch array replaces shorter base array
R1 — Same-length arrays — patch wins inteiramente
R1 — Base empty, patch non-empty
R1 — Base non-empty, patch empty — result is empty array
R1 — Array under one object level
R1 — Array under two object levels
R1 — Arrays of objects without ids — replace, NOT recursive merge by index
R1 — Arrays of primitives — replace
R1 — Arrays of mixed types — replace
R2 — Base has array at key, patch has object at same key (no $ops) → patch wins
R2 — Base has object at key, patch has array at same key → patch wins
R2 — Object → primitive
R2 — Array → primitive
R2 — Primitive → object
R2 — Primitive → array
R2 top-level — Base is array, patch is object → patch wins
R2 top-level — Base is object, patch is array → patch wins
R2 top-level — Base is array, patch is primitive → patch wins
R2 top-level — Base is object, patch is primitive → patch wins
Null base + object patch → object wins
Object base + null patch → null wins
Nested null base, object patch — object wins at that key
Nested object base, null patch — null wins at that key
R4 — $ops with single positional add at end
R4 — $ops with single positional add at start
R4 — $ops with single positional remove (no redundant `item`)
R4 — positional remove at index 0
R4 — positional remove at last index
R4 — multiple positional removes in same $ops (tests descending-index application)
R4 — remove every element via $ops → empty array
R4 — $ops adding into empty base
R4 — Combined positional remove + add in single $ops
R4 — $ops with positional move (no redundant `item`)
R4 — Smart-key remove standalone (no add pair, no move)
R4 — Multiple smart-key removes in same $ops
R4 — Smart-key remove + nested update of another (surviving) item in same patch
R4 — Smart-key remove for a key that doesn't exist. Normal: silent ignore. Strict: throw KEY_NOT_FOUND.
R8 — Pure smart-key update with no add/remove/move still uses `$ops: []` as the array-diff discriminator. Without the empty `$ops`, the diff would be interpreted as an object merge.
R4 — Smart-key move (canonical form): `{type:'move', key, to}` using default identity `id`
R4 sugar — `remove key=X` + `add key=X` is equivalent to a smart-key move; item identity is preserved (default identity `id`)
R4 — Smart-key with nested object updates alongside add of new item (default identity `id`)
Positional $ops works uniformly on heterogeneous arrays — any JSON value is a valid item; index is the only identity.
Smart-key ops are silently scoped to objects with `id`/`key`. Non-objects (strings, numbers, booleans) are invisible to the lookup — preserved in place.
Smart-key add into an all-strings array — patch uses default identity `id`. The new item is built as `{ id, ...seed }`.
R5 — $remove with single key in list
R5 — $remove with multiple keys in same list
R5 — $remove scoped to nested object level
R5 — $remove + add new keys in same patch
R5 — $remove processed BEFORE other entries: `{$remove:['a'], a:99}` removes then re-adds → effectively reset
R5 — $remove with empty list is a no-op
R5 — $remove with keys that don't exist in base. Normal: silent ignore. Strict: throw OBJECT_KEY_NOT_FOUND.
R5 — Realistic — $remove at deep level + regular merge at sibling
Realistic merge — deep object update without arrays
Realistic merge — array under nested object is replaced (no ops declared)
Realistic merge — $ops on one field, plain object merge on sibling

Canonical mixed-merge pattern — scalar properties merge normally at the parent object level; an array field uses a `$ops` wrapper to declare structural ops + smart-key nested updates for the items it contains. The `$ops` marker NEVER coexists with regular scalar properties at the same object level — siblings of `$ops` are always smart-key references to items in the resulting array.
R7 — positional remove with index past array length. Normal: silent skip. Strict: throw INDEX_OUT_OF_RANGE.
R7 — positional move with `from` past array length. Normal: silent skip. Strict: throw INDEX_OUT_OF_RANGE.
R7 — smart-key move for a key that doesn't exist. Normal: silent skip. Strict: throw KEY_NOT_FOUND.
R7 — positional move where `from === to`. Normal: silent skip. Strict: throw MOVE_NO_OP (sintoma de bug do gerador).
R7 — smart-key move where the item is already at the target index. Strict: throw MOVE_NO_OP.
R7 — smart-key add where the key is already present in the array. Normal: silently inserts duplicate. Strict: throw KEY_ALREADY_EXISTS.
R7 — nested update (sibling key of $ops) doesn't match any smart-key in the resulting array. Normal: silent ignore. Strict: throw KEY_NOT_FOUND.
R9 — Array-diff declares `$identity: "code"` in the wire; patcher uses `code` for smart-key lookups within this array.
R9 — Same document has two arrays with DIFFERENT identity fields. Each array-diff declares its own `$identity` in the wire.
R9 — Add op with `$identity: "code"` builds the new item using `code` as the identity field.
Gap C fix — when adding an item via smart-key, the original type of the identity field is preserved (number stays number).
Gap B fix — first occurrence wins smart-key, others fall back to content hash. Changing only the second still applies correctly.
R10 — `$assertCollection: true` with a valid homogeneous base: every item is an object with `id`, no duplicates. Passes validation and applies normally.
R10 — `$assertCollection: true` with a primitive item in the base array → throw COLLECTION_NON_OBJECT_ITEM.
R10 — `$assertCollection: true` with an object lacking the identity field → throw COLLECTION_MISSING_IDENTITY.
R10 — `$assertCollection: true` with two items sharing the same identity value → throw COLLECTION_DUPLICATE_IDENTITY.
R10 — `$assertCollection: true` honors the local `$identity`. Validation uses the declared identity field.
R10 — When `$identity: "sku"` is declared with `$assertCollection: true`, an object lacking `sku` triggers the assertion (NOT objects missing `id`).
R11 — Single cell edit in a 2D matrix via two nested `$identity: ":index"` levels. Sibling key `"1"` routes to result[1], then `"2"` to result[1][2].
R11 — Nd recursion. Single cell edit in a 2×2×2 cube via three nested `$identity: ":index"` levels.
R11 — Positional `$ops` add inserts a new row into a matrix. Pure structural growth, no nested updates.
R11 — Positional `$ops` remove drops a row from a matrix.
R11 — Composition: add a new row via `$ops` AND edit a cell in an existing row in the same patch. Nested-update sibling key indexes the RESULT (post-ops).
R11 — Matrix nested inside an object — sibling identity is local to the array level. Object-level recursion above; positional below.

R11 — Composition with smart-key: a cell holds an object that recurses via plain-object merge (no `$ops`). The smart-key value of the cell does NOT participate in lookup — the cell is selected by positional sibling key.
R11 — Out-of-range sibling index: normal mode silently skips (returns base unchanged), strict mode throws KEY_NOT_FOUND.
R11 — Non-integer sibling key on a positional array: normal mode silently skips, strict mode throws KEY_NOT_FOUND.
R11 — `$assertCollection: true` is silenced in positional mode (a matrix's rows are arrays, not identity-bearing objects). Patch applies normally instead of throwing COLLECTION_NON_OBJECT_ITEM.
R6 — Patch declares $ops but base at that position is an object. Fail-fast: throw OPS_BASE_NOT_ARRAY.
R6 — Patch declares $ops but base at that position is a primitive. Fail-fast: throw.
R6 — Patch declares $ops for a key that doesn't exist in base. Missing is not array → throw.
